title: Encephalitozoon cuniculi in rabbits
subtitle: What this common rabbit parasite does, how to recognise an acute episode, and what treatment involves.
excerpt: E. cuniculi is a microsporidian parasite carried asymptomatically by a large proportion of domestic rabbits. When it triggers clinical disease, the signs appear suddenly: a head tilt, rolling, or rapid eye movement that looks like a neurological emergency. Most rabbits recover substantially with treatment, but the episode is distressing for owners who do not know what they are seeing.
sourceNotes: Varga (2013), Textbook of Rabbit Medicine (2nd ed.), Butterworth-Heinemann: E. cuniculi chapter. Harcourt-Brown and Holloway (2003): Encephalitozoon cuniculi in pet rabbits, Veterinary Record. RCVS Knowledge: companion animal parasite guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A persistent lateral rotation of the head in which the rabbit holds its neck twisted to one side. In rabbits, head tilt is most commonly caused by E. cuniculi affecting the brain, or by otitis interna (middle and inner ear infection). The two causes can look identical from the outside; a vet examination and response to treatment help distinguish them.

An antiparasitic drug licensed for use in rabbits at a higher dose rate than for other species, used for treatment and prevention of E. cuniculi. A 28-day course is used for acute E. cuniculi episodes; a 9-day annual course is used for prophylaxis in at-risk or multi-rabbit households. Obtainable on veterinary prescription.

An inflammatory condition of the eye in rabbits caused by E. cuniculi spores infecting the lens. The lens ruptures, releasing protein into the eye, causing severe uveitis. Presents as a white or opaque lens, often with a cloudy or reddened eye. Treatment in early cases involves anti-inflammatory medication; enucleation (removal of the eye) is required if the inflammation is uncontrolled.

content.text: E. cuniculi is extremely common in domestic rabbits: seroprevalence studies suggest 50 to 80% of pet rabbits have been exposed. Most never show clinical signs. In a minority, the parasite causes sudden neurological disease, kidney damage, or eye problems. Understanding the disease prevents unnecessary panic during an acute episode and helps owners seek the right treatment promptly.

The most dramatic presentation of E. cuniculi is sudden neurological collapse: the rabbit rolls, cannot right itself, has rapid uncontrolled eye movements (nystagmus), and may seem to be fitting. This is usually an acute episode triggered by inflammation in the brain stem or cerebellum from EC cysts rupturing. It is distressing to observe. A rabbit in this state should be wrapped in a towel to prevent it injuring itself on hard surfaces and taken to a vet the same day.
A rabbit that survives the acute phase and receives treatment often improves substantially within days, though a permanent residual
is common after a bad episode. Rabbits with a stable head tilt can live well and manage normal activities.
A vet will typically treat an acute EC episode with

for 28 days, combined with meloxicam to calm swelling during the acute phase. Medication to reduce rolling and eye movement may be given at the start. At home, syringe-feed the rabbit if it cannot reach its bowl, and separate it briefly from a bonded companion if the companion is causing distress.
A white or cloudy appearance to the lens in one or both eyes, with or without neurological signs, may indicate

. This is painful and requires prompt veterinary treatment. A rabbit with a white eye that is also rubbing or pawing at the eye, holding it closed, or has reduced activity needs veterinary assessment quickly.
